title: The Financing Mismatch That Turns Growth Into a Permanent Cash Squeeze
linkedin:
Profitable business. Growing revenue. Overdraft permanently sitting near its limit anyway.

We see this pattern a lot, and it rarely gets named because every individual decision that caused it made sense at the time.

A van gets bought on the overdraft while the asset finance application is still with the broker. A hire made during a quiet month gets carried through on the facility, and by the time trade picks up, that headcount is just part of the business. None of it feels like a financing decision in the moment.

Eighteen months later, the revolving facility that used to move up and down with the trading cycle never comes back down. The instinct is to call it a cash flow problem.

Most of the time it isn't. It's a financing mismatch. Short-term debt, built to absorb a timing gap that reverses within weeks, has quietly ended up carrying a van, a fit-out, or a permanent team increase, none of which were ever going to pay back inside a single trading cycle.

The fix isn't more cash. It's matching the type of capital to the job it's actually doing: revolving facilities for genuine short-term gaps, term debt or asset finance for anything with a multi-year life, and growth capital for the step-changes that don't have collateral or an immediate payback behind them.

Mid-July, with last financial year closed and this year's spending not yet locked in, is a clean moment to check which of your facilities is quietly carrying the wrong kind of weight.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #FractionalCFO #SMEFinance
facebook:
Ever notice your overdraft never quite comes back down, even in a good month?

It's rarely a trading problem. More often, a van or a new hire got funded on the overdraft because that was the easy option at the time, and it just never got moved onto proper term finance.

Short-term facilities are built for short-term gaps, the wait between paying a supplier and getting paid yourself. Vehicles, fit-outs and permanent team growth belong on a different kind of debt entirely, one matched to how long that spending will actually be earning its keep.

Has your business ever caught itself carrying a purchase on the wrong facility for way longer than planned? We'd love to hear how you handled it.

#CashFlow
google:
A note for East Coast business owners: if your overdraft or line of credit never quite comes back to zero even in a strong trading month, it's worth checking what's actually sitting on it.

A common pattern we see is a vehicle, a fit-out, or a new hire getting funded through a short-term facility simply because it was already open and available, with the plan to refinance onto proper term debt later. Later often doesn't arrive, and that facility quietly becomes permanent, leaving no real buffer for the short-term shocks it was originally there to absorb, a late-paying customer or an unexpectedly quiet month.

The good news is this is straightforward to check. Look at what's currently drawn on your overdraft or line of credit and ask honestly whether each item is a genuine short-term timing gap or something that has effectively become permanent. Anything permanent is usually a candidate for refinancing onto a facility with a term that matches how long it will actually be earning revenue.

Mid-July, with the last financial year closed and this year's spending not yet fully underway, is a sensible time to run this check before another year of growth gets funded the same way. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog for the detail on how to tell the difference and what to do about it.
